title: Carphobolus umbellulatus
intro: Carphobolus umbellulatus, also known as Devil's Claw, is a plant species native to southern Africa, characterized by its unique claw-shaped fruit. The plant is valued for its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, offering potential health benefits in the management of pain and inflammation, particularly in conditions such as arthritis and fibromyalgia. Bioactive constituents of Carphobolus umbellulatus include harpagoside, harpagide, and procumbide, which have been identified as the primary compounds responsible for the plant's medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Devil's Claw include capsules, tablets, and teas, which are often marketed as dietary supplements for the relief of pain and discomfort.
taxonomy: Carphobolus umbellulatus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae. It belongs to the genus Carphobolus. Within the plant kingdom, it is classified as a dicotyledonous angiosperm. Further classification places it in the clade Asterids. At the rank of kingdom, it is classified as Plantae.
uses: Carphobolus umbellulatus is a plant species that has been studied for its potential medicinal properties, although more research is needed to confirm its uses. Its extracts have been found to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which may contribute to its potential health benefits. Traditional medicine has used plants in this genus for various purposes, including treating digestive issues and skin conditions. However, the specific medicinal uses of Carphobolus umbellulatus are not well documented, and more studies are necessary to understand its effects on human health. Further research is required to unlock the full potential of this plant for medicinal purposes.
properties: Carphobolus umbellulatus is a plant species whose therapeutic properties are not well-documented.
